title: How to Interpret Your Child's Standardized Test Score Report Without a Statistics Degree
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You receive your child's standardized test score report. It contains numbers, percentiles, growth scores, and bands of various widths. The language is technical and impersonal. You know these scores are supposed to tell you how your child is doing, but you are not sure what to make of them. Is a score at the 55th percentile good or concerning? Should you worry about the difference between the math score and the reading score? What does it mean that your child grew four points since last year? You are not alone in your confusion. These reports are often designed for educators and policymakers, not for parents. Here is how to read them and what the numbers actually mean for your child.

The Different Types of Scores and What They Mean

A standardized test report typically contains several different types of scores. The raw score is the number of questions your child answered correctly. It is rarely reported directly because it is not meaningful on its own. A raw score of thirty out of forty means something very different on an easy test than on a hard test.

The scaled score converts the raw score to a consistent scale that allows comparisons across different versions of the test. A scaled score of 650 on one administration of the test should represent the same level of achievement as a 650 on another administration. Scaled scores are useful for tracking growth over time. If your child's scaled score increased from 620 to 645, they made progress. The scaled score does not tell you how your child compares to other students.

The percentile rank tells you how your child performed relative to a norm group of students at the same grade level. A score at the 55th percentile means your child scored higher than 55 percent of students in the norm group. Percentiles are often misinterpreted. A score at the 50th percentile is not a failing grade. It is exactly average. Half of all students score below this point by definition. A score at the 25th percentile is below average but still within the broad range of normal. A score below the 10th percentile may indicate a need for additional support.

The confidence band is the range within which your child's true score likely falls. No test measures ability perfectly. If your child had taken the test on a different day, with slightly different questions, their score would likely be different. The confidence band acknowledges this uncertainty. A reported score of 650 with a confidence band of 630 to 670 means that your child's true ability is likely somewhere in that range. When comparing two scores, if the confidence bands overlap, the difference may not be meaningful. A gain of five points that falls entirely within the margin of error is not strong evidence of improvement.

Growth scores attempt to measure how much your child has improved over time. A growth percentile compares your child's growth to the growth of students with similar score histories. A growth percentile of 60 means your child grew more than 60 percent of students who started at a similar level. This is different from an achievement percentile, which compares your child to all students. A child can be at a low achievement percentile but a high growth percentile, indicating that they are behind but catching up. This is a positive signal that raw achievement scores would miss.

How to Read the Report as a Parent

Start with the big picture. Is your child performing at, above, or below grade-level expectations? Most reports include a designation like meets standards, approaches standards, or exceeds standards. This designation is a summary judgment based on the scaled score. A child who meets standards is performing at the level expected for their grade. This is a good outcome. You do not need your child to exceed standards in every category. Meeting standards across the board is a solid academic foundation.

Look for patterns across subjects. Is your child consistently stronger in reading than in math, or vice versa? A moderate difference is normal. Most people have relative strengths and weaknesses. A large difference, such as reading at the 80th percentile and math at the 20th percentile, may indicate a specific learning difficulty that warrants further investigation. The pattern over multiple test administrations is more informative than a single data point.

Look at growth over time. A single year's scores are a snapshot. Multiple years of scores are a movie. Is your child's percentile rank stable, rising, or declining? A declining trend over two or three years is worth investigating, even if the scores are still above average. A rising trend is a positive signal, even if the scores are still below average. The trajectory matters as much as the current position.

Do not obsess over small changes. A five-point increase or a shift of a few percentile points is often within the margin of error. Do not celebrate it as a major victory or panic about it as a concerning decline. Look for meaningful changes: shifts of fifteen or more percentile points, or scores that cross the boundary from meeting standards to not meeting standards.

When to Be Concerned

A single low score is not a crisis. Children have bad days. They rush through sections. They misunderstand instructions. If the score is inconsistent with your observations of your child's abilities and with their classroom performance, trust the broader picture rather than the single data point.

A pattern of low scores across multiple test administrations is worth investigating. Request a conference with your child's teacher. Bring the score reports and ask whether the teacher sees similar patterns in classroom work. If the teacher is also concerned, discuss what interventions are available. The school may recommend additional assessment to determine whether there is an underlying learning issue.

Scores that decline significantly over time also warrant investigation. A child who was meeting standards and is now approaching or below standards may be struggling with new material, experiencing social or emotional difficulties, or dealing with a change in circumstances that is affecting their academic performance.

The Bottom Line

Standardized test score reports are dense and technical, but the core information is accessible if you know what to look for. Focus on whether your child meets grade-level standards, whether there are large discrepancies between subjects, and whether the trend over time is positive or negative. Do not obsess over small fluctuations. A single score is a snapshot. Multiple scores over time tell the real story. If the story raises concerns, talk to your child's teacher. The test report is one piece of data about your child's academic development. It is not the whole picture.
